titleStmt.title: Pairing Symmetries of Unconventional High Temperature Superconductivity in a Zinc-Blende Structure

funder.orgName: National Natural Science Foundation of China
of the Chinese Academy of Sciences

affiliation.orgName: Beijing National Laboratory for Condensed Matter Physics
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
Beijing National Laboratory for Condensed Matter Physics

Beijing National Laboratory for Condensed Matter Physics
Beijing National Laboratory for Condensed Matter Physics
Beijing National Laboratory for Condensed Matter Physics
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

analytic.title: Pairing Symmetries of Unconventional High Temperature Superconductivity in a Zinc-Blende Structure

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We classify the pairing symmetries of three-dimensional superconductivity in the zinc-blende structure which can support an electronic environment to host unconventional high temperature superconductivity, and calculate the pairing symmetry in the presence of strong electron-electron correlation by the slave boson mean-field approach. We find that the d 2z 2 -x 2 -y 2 ± id x 2 -y 2 pairing state, a three dimensional analogy of the d ± id pairing in a two dimensional square lattice, is ubiquitously favored near half filling upon hole doping in both single-orbital and three-orbital models. However, unlike the two dimensional counterpart, the Bogoliubov quasiparticle spectrum of the three dimensional state upholds the full T d point group symmetry and encompasses point nodes along certain high symmetric lines.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: We recently proposed a new family of unconventional high-temperature superconductors in the zincblende structure, guided by the "gene" concept of hightemperature superconductors [1]. This theory emphasizes that the d-orbitals of the cations involved in strong hybridization and superexchange should be isolated around the Fermi surface. In contrast to the two well-known examples, namely cuprates and iron-based superconductors [2][3][4][5][6], the new family of superconductors is primarily three-dimensional in nature. Typically, the orbitals in the 3d-shell of transition metal atoms extend in one or two dimensions. Consequently, it is commonly believed that strong correlation and hightemperature effects occur in quasi-two dimensions. However, there can be exceptions to this generalization, where specific cation-anion local environments collaborate with the global symmetry. The zinc-blende structure is one such exception. [7].

The zinc-blende structure possesses a space group of F 43m(N o.216) and a corresponding point group of T d . The crystal field splitting effect results in the upward displacement of the three t 2g -orbitals, namely d xy , d yz , and d zx . Consequently, these orbitals exhibit strong hopping interactions in three spatial directions. In the vicinity of half filling (with an electronic configuration approximating d 7 ), this system acts as an unconventional threedimensional high-temperature superconductor. Given the system's high symmetry, the investigation of the superconducting states and their corresponding symmetries in this structure holds great interest.

The investigation of pairing symmetries constrained by lattice symmetries has been a topic of inquiry for a long time [8]. Specifically, in heavy-fermion superconductivity [9][10][11][12], the O h , D 4h , and D 6h point groups have received significant attention due to their corresponding materialspecific properties. However, the T d group, which features the zinc-blende structure, exhibits different pairing symmetries. In Section III, we provide an overview of the representations of the T d group for convenience in subsequent discussion. In the same Section, we employ the phenomenological Landau theory to explore the full range of possible pairing states determined by the lattice symmetry. To determine the ground state in this model, we use the slave-boson mean field method, as discussed in Section IV. Remarkably, the analysis reveals that the most favorable state of superconductivity is a d±id state that breaks time-reversal symmetry but maintains lattice symmetry. This pairing function is a direct three-dimensional analogy to the d ± id pairing found in quasi-two-dimensional twisted double-layer copper oxides [13][14][15][16], but it features zeros along the cubic diagonal lines in the reciprocal lattice, resulting in point nodes in the Bogoliubov quasiparticle spectrum. Lastly, in Section V, we analyze the symmetries of the quasiparticle spectra for various pairing states.

div.head: II. REPRESENTATIONS AND BASIS
div.p: We present the character table and real-space basis functions f i (r) for the T d group in Table I for reference purposes. In our analysis, we focus on the nearest neighbor (NN) hopping and pairing, and as such, the cubic harmonics on the NN bond provide useful insight. Specifically, the zinc-blende structure possesses 12 NN bonds that form lattice harmonics with basis functions Γ i (k) that are classified at the bottom of Table I The NN and second NN cubic harmonics are also listed below as the basis functions of representations, where the NN cµ and sµ represent cos (kµ/2) and sin (kµ/2), respectively, and the second NN is represented by Cµ with its value being cos kµ. The variable µ takes the values of x, y, and z. The symbol t p 2i , where p equals o or e, denotes odd or even parity t2i basis functions.
div.formula: T d E 8C3 3C2 6S4 6σ d basis functions fi(r) A1 1 1 1 1 1 1,x 2 + y 2 + z 2 ,xyz A2 1 1 1 -1 -1 x 4 (y 2 -z 2 ) + y 4 (z 2 -x 2 ) +z 4 (x 2 -y 2 ) E 2 -1 2 0 0 [2z 2 -x 2 -y 2 , √ 3(x 2 -y 2 )] T1 3 0 -1 1 -1 [x(y 2 -z 2 ), y(z 2 -x 2 ), z(x 2 -y 2 )] T2 3 0 -1 -1 1 [x, y, z], [yz, xz, xy] Cubic Harmonics Γi(k) a1 cxcy + cycz + czcx, Cx + Cy + Cz ei [2cxcy -cycz -cxcz, √ 3(cycz -cxcz)], [2Cz -Cx -Cy, √ 3(Cx -Cy)] t1i [isx(cy -cz), isy(cz -cx), isz(cx -cy)] t o 2i [isx(cy + cz), isy(cz + cx), isz(cx + cy)] t e 2i [sysz, sxsz, sxsy] the expression Γ i (k) ∼ j e ik•aj f i (a j ),(1)
div.p: where a j are the 12 NN lattice vectors. We omit the normalized constant and note that the basis lattice vectors are a 1 = (1, 1, 0)/2, a 2 = (1, 0, 1)/2, and a 3 = (0, 1, 1)/2. Among the representation harmonics, we identify that the basis functions t 1i and t o 2i possess spatial parity oddness, and the imaginary factor with the odd parity ensures the time reversal symmetry of the basis functions. The A 2 representation cannot be expressed within the NN harmonics. We can obtain further neighbor cubic harmonics similarly. Specifically, the second NN only has the a 1 (k) and e i (k) forms as given in Table I, whereas the third NN harmonics can be obtained by replacing
div.formula: kµ 2 with k µ in NN harmonics.
div.p: We also tabulate the product of the T d group representations in Table II for future reference. In Section III, we employ the four-fold products of these representations in the free energy, and even six-fold order terms, to distinguish between potentially degenerate superconducting states. In the three-orbital model, the bilinear forms of the orbital component must be decomposed into different representations. This process involves decomposing the T 2 ⊗ T 2 representation, as listed in Table II. Additionally, for the pairing representation in the three-orbital model, we consider the product of the cubic harmonics and the bilinear forms of the orbitals, with the appropriate decomposition listed in Table V.
Energy (ev)

div.head: III. SYMMETRY OF SUPERCONDUCTIVITY
div.p: A. Single-orbital model

For clarity, we start with a single-orbital model. The cation atoms form a face-centered cubic (FCC) structure, which is illustrated in Fig. 1(a) alongside its respective unit vectors. Its point group is O h , which is higher than the T d group for the extra inversion symmetry. Nevertheless, the parity-even representation basis functions and product table coincide with those of T d . The NN hopping can be described by the Hamiltonian H = k,σ â † kσ H 1t âkσ , where σ represents spin-up or spin-down, and
Here
div.formula: A2 E T1 T2 A2 A1 E T2 T1 E E A1 + A2 + E T1 + T2 T1 + T2 T1 T2 T1 + T2 A1 + E + T1 + T2 A2 + E + T1 + T2 T2 T1 T1 + T2 A2 + E + T1 + T2 A1 + E + T1 + T2
div.p: structure and the single-orbital tight binding model's dispersion along high-symmetry lines are demonstrated in Fig. 1 (b) and (c), respectively.

The pairing function belongs to a particular representation of the corresponding group. It is expressed as a linear combination of the cubic harmonics listed in Table.I. In our previous work [7], we only consider the equal pairing amplitude δ a/e/t2 on the NN bonds which only allows , which need to be determined using the phenomenological Ginzburg-Landau theory.

div.head: B. Landau theory
div.p: According to Landau's phase transition theory, symmetries are broken at the critical temperature. In conventional superconductivity, only the U (1) symmetry is broken, while unconventional pairing states can spontaneously break additional symmetries such as time reversal, spin rotation, and point group symmetry. The pairing form can be obtained by solving the mean-field selfconsistent equation, which involves an eigenvalue problem with the gap function representing the corresponding eigenfunction. Pairings belonging to different representations have different transition temperatures. Therefore, each pairing form carries a specific representation and can be expressed as a linear combination of wavefunctions Γ i , corresponding to that representation, with coefficients δ i . At high temperature, these coefficients are zero to fulfill the complete symmetry, while at temperature lower than the critical temperature, their non-zero values break some symmetries.

As a function of the wave-functions, the free energy respects all the symmetries including point group, time reversal, U (1) symmetry and spin rotational SU (2) symmetry. To maintain the U (1) and time reversal symmetries, only real even-order products of δ i 's are allowed, with the second-order term being α i |δ i | 2 . To obtain the fourth-order terms, we extract terms belonging to A 1 representation from Γ⊗Γ * ⊗Γ⊗Γ * . Since this process for A 1 representation is trivial, the E and T 2 representations are considered below.

In the E representation, the pairing form is given by ∆(k) = 2 i=1,2 δ i e i (k). Referring to Table II for the product of representations, we can list three terms belonging to A 1 representation as follows:
The third term is not independent, thus the fourth-order term in the free energy is given by:
We can substitute the pairing parameters (δ 1 , δ 2 ) = δ 0 (cos γ, sin γe iθ ) to rewrite the fourth-order terms in the free energy, which are given by:

In two different cases, the minimum of the free energy corresponds to two stable phases, which are e 1 (k), e 2 (k), or e 1 (k) + ce 2 (k) (c ∈ R) when β 2 < 0, and e 1 (k) ± ie 2 (k) when β 2 > 0 and β 1 -β 2 > 0. Considering only fourth-order terms in the phenomenological theory results in degeneracy between the e 1 state and the e 2 state. This degeneracy can be eliminated by introducing six-order terms in the free energy or through the slave-boson mean field calculation. Additionally, in the case of the T d group, the e 1 /e 2 basis can cyclically rotate among x, y, and z due to the C 3 rotation, leading to a three-fold degeneracy and D 2d subgroup symmetry of the quasiparticle excitation in this state, as shown in Table VI. The time-reversal-symmetry-breaking (TRSB) e 1 ± ie 2 states maintain equal on-bond pairing amplitude, and their Bogoliubov quasiparticle spectra respect the full point group symmetry. This is because the pairing amplitude e 2 1 + e 2 2 belongs to A 1 representation of the T d point group.

div.head: T2 representation
div.p: For the T 2 representation with ∆(k) = 4 i=1-3 δ i t e 2i (k), we can similarly obtain the terms belonging to A 1 representation from the four-representation product Γ ⊗ Γ * ⊗ Γ ⊗ Γ * . These terms are shown below:
They are essentially the polynomial invariants that result from projecting |δ 1 + δ 2 + δ 3 | 4 onto the basis functions. There are three independent terms. Therefore,
The stable phases from Landau theory for T2 representation. The circled numbers denote the time reversal invariant, time reversal broken/chiral, polarized and planar phases, respectively.
the fourth-order terms in free energy can be expressed as follows with some rephrasing:
By normalizing (|δ
, the minimum of the free energy yields the four stable phases depicted in Fig. 2:
div.formula: • (i). Time reversal invariant t 21 ± t 22 ± t 23 , when β 3 < 0, β 2 < 0 and 3β 1 + β 2 + 3β 3 > 0 • (ii). Chiral t 21 ± ωt 22 ± ω * t 23 with ω = e ±i2π/3 , β 3 > 0, β 2 < 0 and 3β 1 + β 2 > 0 • (iii). Polarized (t 21 , t 22 , t 23 ), when β 3 > -β 1 , β 2 > 0 and β 2 -4β 3 > 0 • (iv). Planar t 21 ± it 22 , when β 2 > 0 and β 2 -4β 3 < 0.
div.p: The degeneracy of each of the four stable phases is 4,8,3,and 6 respectively. Both the time reversal invariant (TRI) and chiral phases have equal on-bond-pairing amplitudes. The point group symmetry is always broken in the quasiparticle spectra of all these pairing forms in the T 2 representation. These parameters β i can be affected by the spinfluctuation-feedback effect [17], which can lead to the transition between different phases in E or T 2 representations.

div.head: Tight-binding Model
div.p: We will start by analyzing the symmetry of the three orbitals that form the T 2 basis of the point group before considering pairing forms. The Hamiltonian consists of the kinetic energy and mean field pairing terms, which are bilinear forms of the orbital operators. Thus, they form
div.formula: T 2 T 2 = A 1 E T 1 T 2 representations. We will use the basis ψ † = (d † yz , d † xz , d † xy )
div.p: without the spin and momentum indices, as they respond similarly to the real space basis (x, ŷ, ẑ) under the operations belonging to the point group. Therefore, we can express the representations explicitly in terms of the orbital part: Γi = αβ ψ † α Γ i,αβ ψ β . The matrix forms of the representations are presented in Table III. The three-orbital tight-binding Hamiltonian, denoted as Γ(k) Γ, corresponds to the A 1 representation of the point group. Additionally, the Hamiltonian is Hermitian, eliminating the possibility of t o 2 (k) T2 and yielding four permissible combinations. Thus the Hamiltonian can be expressed as
div.formula: H = k ψ † (k)H 3t (k)ψ(k) with the matrix H 3t (k) = (h 1 a 1 -µ) Â1 + h 2 i e i Êi + h 3 i t e 2i T2i + h 4 i t 1i T1i ,(9)
div.p: where h 1-4 are real NN hopping parameters and µ is the chemical potential. The first two matrix elements are obtained as follow:
div.formula: H 3t,11 (k) = 4 t 1 (c x c y + c x c z ) + t 2 c y c z -µ, H 3t,12 (k) = 4 it 4 s z (c x -c y ) -t 3 s x s y ) .(10)
div.p: Other terms can be obtained by a cyclical permutation of x, y, z. Here, 4(t
to match the real space NN hopping parameters. For example, in a 1 direction, the hopping matrix T (a 1 ) is given by
The matrix T (a 1 ) is invariant under the mirror on the plane ( 110), two-fold rotation along the z-axis, and time reversal.

From the tight-binding model, we can analytically determine the band splitting at high-symmetry momenta: Γ, X, W , K, and L. These splittings can aid in fitting the four hopping parameters, h i . At the Γ point, the energy is always threefold degenerate at 12h 1 . The band splitting at each momenta is as follows: δ X = -48h 2 , δ W = -24h 2 ± 8h 4 , and δ L = -12h 3 . Figure 1 (d) shows the dispersion of the three-orbital tight-binding model along high-symmetry lines with only nearest-neighbor hopping terms. To obtain the hopping parameters and chemical potential listed in Table IV, we fit the results of the density function theory (DFT) for zinc-blende cobalt nitrogen (CoN) as discussed in Ref. [7].
The second NN hopping terms are relatively simple since their harmonics only exhibit A 1 and E representations, contributing solely to the intra-orbital hoppings. This can be expressed as:

The variables are defined as a 1,2 = µ cos(k µ ), e 1,2 = 2 cos(k z ) -cos k x -cos k y , and e 2,2 = cos(k x ) -cos(k y ). The third NN hopping terms are similar to the NN bonds since they share the same symmetries. The only difference is that we should modify the k-dependence of the harmonics, which is obtained by replacing kµ 2 with k µ .

div.head: Pairing part
div.p: The method of classifying the pairing component is similar to that of the tight-binding model discussed earlier. The overall representation of pairing consists of cubic harmonics and orbital bilinear forms, which is represented by Γ(k) ⊗ Γ. In the pairing channels, the operator can be expressed as Γi = αβ ψ † α Γ i,αβ ψ † β , and the matrix form Γ i,αβ is identical to that of the kinetic component because the annihilator and creator transform identically under the point group. Similar to the Hermitian condition of H † t (k) = H t (k) and the time reversal symmetric condition of H * t (k) = H t (-k) for the kinetic component, the pairing term must satisfy the spin-singlet condition of ∆ T (k) = ∆(-k) because our focus is primarily on the antiferromagnetic(AFM)-induced spin-singlet pairing. Here, ∆(k) denotes the pairing matrix in the ψ † k basis. Therefore, only the basis functions with odd parity, such as t o 2i (k) and t 1i (k) listed in Table I, can combine with anti-symmetric operators T1i .

Table V presents the complete representation decomposition of the nearest-neighbor (NN) singlet pairing. The A 1 channel is the same as that of the tight-binding part, where the hopping parameters h i are replaced by pairing parameters δ A1 i . The entire E pairing channel comprises six parameters, namely δ E i . These include three intra-orbital pairing forms and three inter-orbital forms. The T 2 representation pairing channel involves seven free parameters δ T2 i , including two intra-orbital forms. Notably, due to the combination of orbital bilinear forms and cubic harmonics, the three-orbital model also contains A 2 and T 1 pairing forms.

In Sec.III B, we listed the stable phases of different representations in the one-orbital model. These phases remain the same in the three-orbital model as we consider the representation under the group theory, except for the additional pairing representations of A 2 and T 1 . The A 2 representation is one-dimensional, resulting in a unique stable phase, while the T 1 representation shares the stable phases of T 2 due to the same phenomenological Landau theory. The stable e ± ie states are enumerated as ∆ E± (k) = j δ E j ( Ê1,j ± i Ê2,j ), with Êi,j representing the i-th basis function of the j-th E-representation basis in Table V. To provide clarity, we present an example: Ê1,4 = 2t 13 T13 -t 11 T11 -t 12 T12 , (13) Ê2,4 = √ 3(t 11 T11 -t 12 T12 ). ( 14)

div.head: IV. SLAVE-BOSON MEAN FIELD METHOD A. Single-orbital Superconductivity
div.p: We employed a single-orbital t-J model [18] at halffilling and doped it to investigate the phases of superconductivity, as illustrated in Fig. 3. The NN spin-singlet pairing of the one-orbital model only allows A 1 , E, and T 2 forms as the spatial component must be even. We set the AFM exchange interaction to J/t = 0.3, 0.5 in Fig. 3. (a,c,e) and (b,d,f), respectively. The energy savings from (a) different pairing terms belonging to the E representation are listed in (a,b), and the results of the terms belonging to the T 2 representation are displayed in (c,d). We compared the lowest energy states from different representations in (e,f). Specifically, for the E representation, the pairing form factors of E , where ω = e ±i2π/3 , and a ̸ = b belong to (1, 2, 3), respectively.

The multi-component superconductivity generally exhibits lower ground state energy in the time reversal symmetry breaking states. In the E representation, the d±id wave has the lowest ground state energy. The states from the T 2 representation have nearly indistinguishable energy at a weak exchange interaction of J/t = 0.3, as shown in (c). However, with stronger antiferromagnetic (AFM) exchange interaction in (d), the chiral states T 2,2 have the lowest ground state energy. Notably, the d ± id wave from the E representation maintains the lowest ground state energy upon hole doping, as indicated in (e,f).

div.head: B. Three-orbital Superconductivity
div.p: In the three-orbital model, there are multiple pairing parameters in different channels. However, since the inter-orbital pairs generally require more energy to condense, we consider only the pairing with the matrix forms of Â1 and Ê, which refer to intra-orbital channels. In particular, Â1 signifies the isotropic pairing in the orbital space, which dominates the pairing state. Figure 4(a) illustrates the pairing amplitudes

2 , and δ E3 3 , which correspond to e 1 Ê1 + e 2 Ê2 , (a 1 Ê), (e 1 Ê1 -e 2 Ê2 , e 1 Ê2 +e 2 Ê1 ), and (t e 21 ( Ê1 -√ 3 Ê2 ), t e 22 ( Ê1 + √ 3 Ê2 ), 2t e 23 Ê1 ), respectively. The pairing amplitudes of these anisotropic intra-orbital pairing channels from different representations attenuate rapidly around halffilling upon hole doping. Thus, for the A 1 , E, and T 2 representations, we focus only on the isotropic intra-orbital pairing channels. The notations of the pairing channels used in single orbital (Table VI) can likewise apply to isotropic intra-orbital counterparts in the three-orbital case, since the operator parts of the isotropic intra-orbital pairing channels are always Â1 .

The AFM exchange interaction was set at J = 0.1 eV, approximately half of the hopping strength and 1/24 of the bandwidth. In Figure 4 (b), we compared the ground state energy of the A 1 , A 2 , and the three E states. The two one-dimensional representations possess ground state energies that are nearly degenerate, but neither of them is preferred. The previously degenerate (E 1 ,E 2 ) states are now separated, with the E 2 state possessing lower ground state energy. In Figure 4 (c), the four states from the T 2 representation listed in Table VI are illustrated. They all have almost indistinguishable energies, with slightly lower energies in the TRSB states (chiral and planar). In (d), we compared the lowest energy states in each representation channel. The results demonstrate that the E 3 (d ± id) state is the most energetically favorable.

div.head: V. QUASIPARTICLE EXCITATION
div.p: For the single-orbital pairing channel, the quasiparticle spectra are simply ± H 2 1t (k) + |∆(k)| 2 , where H 1t (k) is the single-orbital normal state dispersion function that is proportional to A 1 representation, and invariant under all point group transformations. Therefore, the symmetry of |∆(k)| 2 governs the quasiparticle spectra, as listed in Table VI. The enumeration includes the states in each representation and their degeneracy, the symmetry of quasiparticle excitations, zeros of the pairing forms, and the manifolds of time reversal. It should be noted that for E representation, the O(2) symmetry of ∆F E 4 (δ) in Eq.( 5) allows any continuous rotation of the basis (e 1 , e 2 ), rendering the combination of e 1 ± ce 2 with any real constant c to be stable according to Landau's phenomenological theory. In addition, when c changes from 0 to √ 3, the subgroup describing the symmetry of the quasipar-TABLE VI. Spectra symmetry of single-orbital pairing channels. The column with identifier i represents the state label. "Deg." abbreviates degeneracy levels, and "Subg" denotes the subgroup symmetry of quasiparticle excitation. The designation "Zeros" indicates the pairing form's zeros. "F" and "L" refer to a two-dimensional face and a one-dimensional line, respectively. The abbreviation "TR" signifies the time reversal symmetry condition. "B" denotes the overall bulk, whereas "F" refers to a specific face. For the three-orbital case, due to the complexity induced by interorbital pairing or anisotropic intra-orbital pairing, the symmetries of the quasiparticle spectra can be different with |∆( ⃗ k)| 2 . Here we are also interested in the d ± id and chiral-T 2 states, since they are favored according to the mean-field calculations in Sec.IV. The intersections of the zero lines and the three orbital normal state Fermi surfaces lead to point nodes on quasiparticle spectra, which are shown in Fig. 6. Their density of states are shown in Fig. 5 (b). The symmetry of quasiparticle spectra is listed in Table .VII. In this table, for multi-orbital d ± id and chiral-T 2 superconductivity, we also consider the inter-orbital pairing channels and anisotropic intra-orbital pairing channels, and we find that they break more crystal symmetry than isotropic intra-orbital channels. The symmetries of the quasiparticle spectra of the isotropic intra-orbital pairing channels are the same as |∆( ⃗ k)| 2 , which is consistent with that in the single-orbital case.
div.formula: i ∆(k) Deg. Subg. Zeros TR E1 e1 3 D 2d F B E2 e2 3 D 2d F B E3 e1 ± ie2 2 T d L F E4 e1 + ce2 3 D 2(d) L B/F T2,

div.head: VI. CONCLUSION
div.p: In summary, we classified the spin-singlet pairing superconductivity in the zinc-blende structure. The d 2z 2 -x 2 -y 2 ± id x 2 -y 2 pairing state, as the threedimensional analogy of the d ± id pairing in a two dimensional square lattice, was found to be the most stable superconducting phase near half-filling upon holedoping for both single-and three-orbital models through the slave-boson mean field method. Nonetheless, d ± id pairings in three dimensions possess line zeroes along the four bulk diagonal lines of a cube, leading to point nodes in quasiparticle spectra. We analyzed the symmetries of quasiparticle spectra belonging to various representations. We found that the quasiparticle spectra of the TRSB d ± id(E 3 ) pairing in the single-orbital case and the isotropic intra-orbital d ± id (e Â1 ) pairing in the three-orbital case maintain the full pointgroup symmetry. The signal of the time-reversal symmetry breaking in this three-dimensional superconductivity can be detected by muon spin relaxation (µSR) measurements [19]. The point nodes can impact the behavior of the specific heat below the superconducting transition temperature [20] and the temperaturedependence of the superfluid density [21], both of which can be observed in experiments.

figure.head: FIG. 1 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. (a). The face center cubic (FCC) structure and the unit vectors in real space. (b). The Brillouin zone of FCC structure and the high-symmetry lines. (c). The dispersion of the single-orbital tight binding model along high-symmetry lines. (d). The dispersion of the three-orbital tight binding model along high-symmetry lines.

figure.head: T 2, 2 FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. Energy comparison of various superconducting states for single-orbital t-J around half-filling upon hole-(x) doping. The exchange strength is set as J/t = 0.3, 0.5 in (a,c,e) and (b,d,f), respectively. The stable states of the E representation are compared in (a,c) and of the T2 representation are in (b,d).

ticle transforms from D 2d (z) to D 2d (x). For arbitrary real value of c, the subgroup symmetry is D 2(d) , and it is time reversal invariant throughout the bulk. Regarding the d±id (E 3 ) and chiral-T 2 (T 2,2 ) states, they both have lower ground state energy near half-filling while possessing zero lines and time reversal invariant planes. The zero lines of the d ± id state are the four bulk diagonal lines of a cube: |k x | = |k y | = |k z |. The intersections of these zero lines with the normal state Fermi surface

figure.head: FIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4. The results for three-orbital t-J model around half filling upon hole doping-x. (a) The strength of the anisotropic pairing parameters from different representations. (b,c,d) The energy savings for different superconducting states. The AFM exchange J = 0.1 eV are adopted. We use the same notation as with the single orbital case to designate the different pairing states that belong to each representation, since the isotropic intra-orbital pairing channels are dominate, and the matrix forms of their operator parts are always Â1.

figure.head: FIG. 5 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 5. The Density of states (DOS) of the d±id and chiral-T2 superconductivity for (a). single-orbital case and (b). threeorbital case, respectively.
. Here, Γ denotes the representation symbol with values a 1 , e, t 1 , and t

row.cell: o/e 2 , and the basis functions are given by

figure.head: TABLE I .
figure.label: I
figure.figDesc: Character table and basis functions for T d point group.

figure.head: TABLE II .
figure.label: II
figure.figDesc: Product table for T d point group

figure.head: TABLE IV .
figure.label: IV
figure.figDesc: The parameters of the three-orbital tight binding model obtained by fitting the density function theory's (DFT's) results of the zinc-blende cobalt nitrogen (CoN).

figure.head: TABLE V .
figure.label: V
figure.figDesc: Nearest neighbor (NN) singlet pairing representation basis. The implicit representation basis (Γi(k) Γj) can be obtained analogously.

row.cell: a1 Â1, e1 Ê1 + e2 Ê2, i t e 2i T2i, i t1i T1i
e1 Ê2 -e2 Ê1, i t o 2i T1i

figure.figDesc: 1 , E 2 , and E 3 are denoted as e 1 , e 2 , and e 1 ±ie 2 , respectively. As for the T 2 representations, the pairing form factors of T 2,1 , T 2,2 , T 2,3 , and T 2,4

row.cell: are t e 21 ± t e 22 ± t e 23 , t e 21 ± ωt e 22 ± ω 2 t e 23 , one of (t e 21 , t e 22 , t e 23 ), and t e 2a + it e 2b

div.p: Acknowledgement: Q.Zhang contributed to this work when he was in IOP. This work is supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (Grant No. 2022YFA1403901, No.2022YFA1403800), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. NSFC-11888101, No. NSFC12174428), the Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. XDB28000000, XDB33000000), the New Cornerstone Science Foundation, and the Chinese Academy of Sciences through the Youth Innovation Promotion Association (Grant No. 2022YSBR-048).

monogr.title: International Journal of Modern Physics B
